description: The Thomas A. Edison Papers makes the documentary legacy of America’s most prolific inventor and innovator accessible through selective, annotated digital image and book editions. In addition, we disseminate our research through journal and book publications, conference papers, information on our website, media appearances, public lectures, museum collaborations, and educational programs. Most of the documents come from the extensive archives of the Thomas Edison National Historical Park and were scanned from our microfilm edition, which you can view as one of our subcollections. Another subcollection consists of over 1,000 notebooks kept by Edison and his laboratory staff. In the future we plan to add documents from nearly 150 other repositories and private collections. The edition will eventually include Edison's 1,093 U.S. Patents and over 500 motion picture catalogs produced by American producers and distributors between 1894 and 1908.  Documents in the Edison Papers encompass a period of rapid industrialization and growth in the scale of enterprise from the Civil War to the onset of the Great Depression. In addition to Edison’s well-known contributions to the development of electric light and power, sound recording, and motion pictures, they also document his contributions to many other industries; including telecommunications (telegraph and telephone), electric batteries, mining, chemical production, cement manufacture, and office and home appliances. They include records of his pioneering laboratories and of the numerous companies he established at home and abroad. In addition, they provide extensive documentation of his family and of his role as a symbol of American ingenuity. The Thomas A. Edison Papers Digital Edition materials are derived from physical documents housed in the Thomas A. Edison Historical Park and over one hundred other repositories and private collections, and are made accessible to the public as historical records. However, some material may represent positions, language, values and stereotypes that are not consistent with the values of the Thomas A. Edison Papers and Rutgers University.  We have retained this language for historical accuracy or to document the issues and social context of time, attitudes, and opinions of the people who created the material. Examples of this language may include: a. Organization names that include outdated terms b. An individual identifying themselves by a term no longer favored by people within that group c. Title or language from manuscripts, published books, articles, films, or songs Our efforts to address offensive and harmful language are ongoing. If you encounter language that is offensive or harmful, or have questions about our statement, please email us at info@taep.rutgers.edu . This statement was adapted from those of Temple University, Drexel University, Colorado University-Boulder, and Wake Forest University.
